subject: Physics
q: The angular momentum of a planet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving around the sun in an elliptical orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>L</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. The magnitude of the areal velocity of the planet is :
question: The angular momentum of a planet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving around the sun in an elliptical orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>L</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. The magnitude of the areal velocity of the planet is :
solution:
For small displacement ds of the planet its area can be written as

dA=12rdl

=12rdsinθ

A.vel =dAdt=12rsinθdsdt=Vrsinθ2

dAdt=12mVrsinθm=L2 m

subject: Physics
q: Two planets, $A$ and $B$ are orbiting a common star in circular orbits of radii $R_A$ and $R_B$, respectively, with $R_B=2 R_A$. The planet $B$ is $4 \sqrt{2}$ times more massive than planet $A$. The ratio $\left(\frac{L_B}{L_A}\right)$ of angular momentum $\left(L_B\right)$ of planet $B$ to that of planet $A\left(L_A\right)$ is closest to integer ________.
question: Two planets, $A$ and $B$ are orbiting a common star in circular orbits of radii $R_A$ and $R_B$, respectively, with $R_B=2 R_A$. The planet $B$ is $4 \sqrt{2}$ times more massive than planet $A$. The ratio $\left(\frac{L_B}{L_A}\right)$ of angular momentum $\left(L_B\right)$ of planet $B$ to that of planet $A\left(L_A\right)$ is closest to integer ________.
solution:
$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{mv}_0 \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}} \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{\mathrm{GMR}}$
here M is mass of star
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{A}}} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}} \\
& =4 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{\frac{2}{1}} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{A}}}=8
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet. If the escape velocity from the earth is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$, the escape velocity in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$ from the planet will be :
question: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet. If the escape velocity from the earth is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$, the escape velocity in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$ from the planet will be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}} \\ & \frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Planet }}}{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Earth }}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{E}}}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}\right)}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Planet }}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Earth }}=5.6 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: What is the minimum energy required to launch a satellite of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> from the surface of a planet of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>M</mtext> </mrow> </math> and radius <math> <mrow> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> in a circular orbit at an altitude of <math> <mrow> <mtext>2R</mtext> </mrow> </math>?
question: What is the minimum energy required to launch a satellite of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> from the surface of a planet of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>M</mtext> </mrow> </math> and radius <math> <mrow> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> in a circular orbit at an altitude of <math> <mrow> <mtext>2R</mtext> </mrow> </math>?
solution:
As we know,
Gravitational potential cnergy =-GMmr
and orbital velocity, v0=GM/R+h
Ef=12mv02-GMm3R=12 mGM3R-GMm3R
=GMm3R12-1=-GMm6R
Ei=-GMmR+K
Ei=Ef
Therefore minimum required energy,
K=5GMm6R

Ve=2GmR …1

subject: Physics
q: Four identical particles of mass M are located at the corners of a square of side ‘a’ . What should be their speed if each of them revolves under the influence of other’s gravitational field in a circular orbit circumscribing the square?
question: Four identical particles of mass M are located at the corners of a square of side ‘a’ . What should be their speed if each of them revolves under the influence of other’s gravitational field in a circular orbit circumscribing the square?
solution:
Gravitational force on each mass 'M'
=2GM2a2+GM22a2=2+12GM2a2
This force will provide required centripetal force
2+12GM2a2=Mv2(a/2)
v=1+122GMa=1.16GMa

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Earth has atmosphere whereas moon doesn’t have any atmosphere.

Reason R: The escape velocity on moon is very small as compared to that on earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Earth has atmosphere whereas moon doesn’t have any atmosphere.

Reason R: The escape velocity on moon is very small as compared to that on earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>  is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocity is given by

ve=2gR   ...(1)

As both the acceleration and radius of the Moon is much less than that of the Earth, the value of the escape velocity in Moon is much less than that on the Earth.

The average velocity of gas molecules remains higher than the escape velocity due to the lower escape velocity on the moon's surface, making the moon unable to maintain an atmosphere.

subject: Physics
q:
A person whose mass is 100 kg travels from Earth to Mars in a spaceship. Neglect all other objects in sky and take acceleration due to gravity on the
surface of the Earth and Mars as 10 m s-2 and 4 m s-2, respectively. Identify from the below figures, the curve that fits best for the weight of the passenger as a function of time.
question:
A person whose mass is 100 kg travels from Earth to Mars in a spaceship. Neglect all other objects in sky and take acceleration due to gravity on the
surface of the Earth and Mars as 10 m s-2 and 4 m s-2, respectively. Identify from the below figures, the curve that fits best for the weight of the passenger as a function of time.
solution: When the passenger moves away from the Earth at some point, gravitational force due to earth will be minimised and then start increasing as the passenger moves towards Mars as g∝1R2 also gravitational force cannot be negative. Therefore, the correct curve that fits best for the weight of the passenger as a function of time is c.

question: The escape velocity of a body depends upon mass as

q: An asteroid is moving directly towards the centre of the earth. When at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>R</mi></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth) from the centre of the earth, it has a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Neglecting the effect of earth's atmosphere, what will be the speed of the asteroid when it hits the surface of the earth (escape velocity from the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math> ? Give your answer to the nearest integer in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>__________.
question: An asteroid is moving directly towards the centre of the earth. When at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>R</mi></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth) from the centre of the earth, it has a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Neglecting the effect of earth's atmosphere, what will be the speed of the asteroid when it hits the surface of the earth (escape velocity from the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math> ? Give your answer to the nearest integer in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>__________.
solution:
KEi+PEi=KEf+PEf
12mu02+-GMm10R=12mv2+-GMmR
v2=u02+2GMR1-110
v=u02+95GMR

=122+9511.222
=144+0.911.22=156.896

=16.028 km s-1

≃16 km s-1

subject: Physics
q:
An object is kept at rest at a distance of 3 R above the earth's surface where R is earth's radius. The minimum speed with which it must be projected so that it does not return to earth is :
(Assume $\mathrm{M}=$ mass of earth, $\mathrm{G}=$ Universal gravitational constant)
question:
An object is kept at rest at a distance of 3 R above the earth's surface where R is earth's radius. The minimum speed with which it must be projected so that it does not return to earth is :
(Assume $\mathrm{M}=$ mass of earth, $\mathrm{G}=$ Universal gravitational constant)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{P}}+\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{k}_0 \\ & -\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{4 \mathrm{R}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{P}}^2=0 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{P}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{2 \mathrm{R}}}
\end{aligned}$
Choice 1

subject: Physics
q: The weight of a body at the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The weight of the body at an altitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> above the earth's surface is (given, radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: The weight of a body at the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The weight of the body at an altitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> above the earth's surface is (given, radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
As height of the object is considerable compared to the radius of the earth, therefore approximation formula can not be used.

So weight at given height,

F=mg'=GMmR+R22=49GMmR2

⇒mg'=49(mg)

=49×18

=8 N

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> in vertically upward direction from the surface of the earth into space. It is given that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is escape velocity and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. If air resistance is considered to the negligible, then the maximum height from the centre of earth, to which the body can go, will be (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> : radius of earth)
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> in vertically upward direction from the surface of the earth into space. It is given that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is escape velocity and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. If air resistance is considered to the negligible, then the maximum height from the centre of earth, to which the body can go, will be (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> : radius of earth)
solution:
Escape velocity is given by, ve=2GMeRe

If the body goes to the height h from the surface of the earth, using mechanical energy conservation,

⇒12mλ2GMeRe2-GMemRe=-GMemRe+h

⇒λ2GMemRe-GMemRe=-GMemRe+h

⇒Re+h=Re1-λ2

q: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform density, the acceleration due to gravity inside the earth at a distance of $r$ from the centre is proportional to
question: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform density, the acceleration due to gravity inside the earth at a distance of $r$ from the centre is proportional to
solution: Acceleration due to gravity at depth d from the surface of the earth or at a distance $r$ from the centre ' $O$ ' of the earth $g^{\prime}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \rho G r$ Hence $g^{\prime} \propto r$

subject: Physics
q: A straight rod of length L extends from x=a to x=L+a. The gravitational force it exerts on a point mass 'm' at x=0, if the mass per unit length of the rod is A+Bx2, is given by:
question: A straight rod of length L extends from x=a to x=L+a. The gravitational force it exerts on a point mass 'm' at x=0, if the mass per unit length of the rod is A+Bx2, is given by:
solution:
F=∫aa+LGm dMx2=Gm∫aa+L​(A+Bx2)dxx2

=Gm∫aa+LAx2dx+∫aa+LB  dx

=Gm[A[-1x]aa+L+BL]

=GmA1a-1a+L+BL

subject: Physics
q: A body weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>, is projected vertically upwards from earth's surface to reach a height above the earth which is equal to nine times the radius of earth. The weight of the body at that height will be:
question: A body weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>, is projected vertically upwards from earth's surface to reach a height above the earth which is equal to nine times the radius of earth. The weight of the body at that height will be:
solution:
Final distance from the centre of earth wil be R+9R=10R.

Now acceleration due to gravity at any height(distance from centre r) is given by,

g=GMr2.

Therefore,

g'=GM10R2

Now, value of g at surface will be, GMR2.

Hence,

g'=g100.

Then, W'=mg'=mg100=W100

subject: Physics
q: If the gravitational field in the space is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>K</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Taking the reference point to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with gravitational potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the gravitational potentials at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in SI unit (Given, that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: If the gravitational field in the space is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>K</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Taking the reference point to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with gravitational potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the gravitational potentials at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in SI unit (Given, that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Given, E=-Kr2

Gravitational field and gravitational potential is related as E=-dVdr

So, -dVdr=-Kr2

Integrating the above relation,

∫10VdV=∫23Kr2dr

⇒V-10=K12-13

⇒V-10=K6⇒V=66+10⇒V=11 J kg-1

Now FC=Fnet

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion $\mathrm{A}$ and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathrm{R}$ Assertion A : The kinetic energy needed to project a body of mass m from earth surface to infinity is $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mgR}$, where R is the radius of earth.
Reason $\mathrm{R}$ : The maximum potential energy of a body is zero when it is projected to infinity from earth surface.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the option given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion $\mathrm{A}$ and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathrm{R}$ Assertion A : The kinetic energy needed to project a body of mass m from earth surface to infinity is $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mgR}$, where R is the radius of earth.
Reason $\mathrm{R}$ : The maximum potential energy of a body is zero when it is projected to infinity from earth surface.

options: Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true and $\mathrm{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$
(4) Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true but $\mathrm{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$
solution:
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{Gm}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=\mathrm{mgR}$
Assertion wrong
at $\infty$
$\mathrm{U}=0$
$\therefore$ Reason correct.

subject: Physics
q: A planet takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> days to complete one revolution around the Sun. If the distance of the planet from Sun is reduced to one fourth of the original distance, how many days will it take to complete one revolution?
question: A planet takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> days to complete one revolution around the Sun. If the distance of the planet from Sun is reduced to one fourth of the original distance, how many days will it take to complete one revolution?
solution:
The Law of Periods: The square of the period of any planet is proportional to the cube of the semimajor axis of its orbit.

T2∝r3

Therefore, we can write

T12r13=T22r23

⇒(200)2r3=T22r43

⇒200×2004×4×4=T22

⇒T2=25 days

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A satellite is moving around earth in the orbit very close to the earth surface. The time period of revolution of satellite depends upon the density of earth.
Statement II: The time period of revolution of the satellite is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{R_{e}}{g}}$ (for satellite very close to the earth surface), where $R_{\mathrm{e}}$ radius of earth and $g$ acceleration due to gravity.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A satellite is moving around earth in the orbit very close to the earth surface. The time period of revolution of satellite depends upon the density of earth.
Statement II: The time period of revolution of the satellite is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{R_{e}}{g}}$ (for satellite very close to the earth surface), where $R_{\mathrm{e}}$ radius of earth and $g$ acceleration due to gravity.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement I: For a satellite very close to Earth's surface, $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{R_e}{g}}$ where $g = \frac{GM}{R_e^2} = \frac{4\pi G \rho R_e}{3}$.
Substituting: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{3}{4\pi G \rho}}$.
This shows period depends on Earth's density $\rho$. Statement I is TRUE.
Statement II: Given directly as $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{R_e}{g}}$, which is the standard formula.
Statement II is TRUE.
Both statements are true.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The radius vector from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time and thus areal velocity of planet is constant.
Reason (R) : For a central force field the angular momentum is a constant.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The radius vector from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time and thus areal velocity of planet is constant.
Reason (R) : For a central force field the angular momentum is a constant.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct and $(\mathrm{R})$ is the correct explanation of $\mathrm{( A)}$
Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
Due to central force torque is zero $\&$ angular momentum is constant.

subject: Physics
q: The time period of a satellite of earth is 5 hours. If the separation between the earth and the satellite is increased to 4 times the previous value, the new time period will become
question: The time period of a satellite of earth is 5 hours. If the separation between the earth and the satellite is increased to 4 times the previous value, the new time period will become
solution:
$\mathrm{T}^2 \propto \mathrm{R}^3 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)^3$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{3 / 2} \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=(4)^{3 / 2}=8$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_2=8 \times \mathrm{T}_1=8 \times 5=40=40$ hours

subject: Physics
q: The mass and the diameter of a planet are three times the respective values for the Earth. The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum on the Earth is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The period of oscillation of the same pendulum on the planet would be:
question: The mass and the diameter of a planet are three times the respective values for the Earth. The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum on the Earth is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The period of oscillation of the same pendulum on the planet would be:
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}$

$\frac{g_{p}}{g_{e}}=\frac{M_{p}}{M_{e}}\left(\frac{R_{e}}{R_{p}}\right)^{2}=3\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{3}$

Also $\mathrm{T} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{g}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{p}}}}=\sqrt{3}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{p}}=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~s}$

subject: Physics
q: The mass of the moon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>144</mn></mfrac></math> times the mass of a planet and its diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>16</mn></mfrac></math> times the diameter of a planet. If the escape velocity on the planet is v, the escape velocity on the moon will be:
question: The mass of the moon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>144</mn></mfrac></math> times the mass of a planet and its diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>16</mn></mfrac></math> times the diameter of a planet. If the escape velocity on the planet is v, the escape velocity on the moon will be:
solution:
As we know, escape velocity is given by, vescape=2GMR

Now, vplanet=2GMR=v

vMoon=2GM×16144×R=132GMR=v3

q: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> from the earth's surface (radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>; <math> <mrow> <mtext>h</mtext> <mtext><<</mtext> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> ). The minimum increase in its orbital velocity required, so that the satellite could escape from the earth's gravitational field, is close to (Neglect the effect of atmosphere.)
question: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> from the earth's surface (radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>; <math> <mrow> <mtext>h</mtext> <mtext><<</mtext> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> ). The minimum increase in its orbital velocity required, so that the satellite could escape from the earth's gravitational field, is close to (Neglect the effect of atmosphere.)
solution:
Since,  h << R ,

Ve=2gR

& V0=gR

∴The minimum increase in velocity required,

Ve-V0=2gR-gR

=2-1gR

subject: Physics
q: If the earth suddenly shrinks to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>64</mn></mfrac><mi>th</mi></math> of its original volume with its mass remaining the same, the period of rotation of earth becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>24</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mi>h</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________
question: If the earth suddenly shrinks to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>64</mn></mfrac><mi>th</mi></math> of its original volume with its mass remaining the same, the period of rotation of earth becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>24</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mi>h</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________
solution:
L= constant; L=Iω

From the conservation of angular momentum,

25MR2ω1=25MR42ω2⇒MR2ω1=MR2ω216⇒ω1ω2=116

Using ω=2πT for each case along with the respective angular frequencies,

T2T1=116

⇒T1T2=161

Using T1=24 h, x=16 by comparing with the given time period in the question.

subject: Physics
q: The escape velocities of two planets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math>. If the ratio of their radii respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the ratio of acceleration due to gravity of planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to the acceleration of gravity of planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be:
question: The escape velocities of two planets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math>. If the ratio of their radii respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the ratio of acceleration due to gravity of planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to the acceleration of gravity of planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be:
solution:
The formula to calculate the mass M of any planet of radius R and density ρ, considering it to be a sphere, is given by

M= 43πR3ρ.....................(1)

The escape velocity vA for planet A can be expressed as

VA=2GMARA=2GρA43πRA3RA=CρA.RA............................(2)

where, G is Gravitational constant and C= 8πG3 is a constant.

Similarly, the escape velocity vB of planet B can be expressed as

vB= CρBRB......................(3)

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) and solve with the known parameters to obtain the ratio of the densities of the planets.

VAVB=CρARACρBRBρAρB= VARBVBRAρAρB= VARBVBRA2= 12×312= 94............................(4)

The acceleration due to gravity gA for planet A is given by

gA=GMARA2=G43πRA3×ρARA2= C22ρARA..........................(5)

Similarly, the acceleration due to gravity gB can be expressed as

gB= C22ρBRB...........................(6)

Divide equation (5) by equation (6) and solve with the help of known parameters to calculate the required ratio of the accelerations due to gravity in both the planets.

gAgB=C22ρARAC22ρBRB=ρAρBRARB= 94×13=34

=14×R2R1=34

subject: Physics
q: The acceleration due to gravity at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> above the earth if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>≪</mo><mi>R</mi></math> (Radius of earth) is given by
question: The acceleration due to gravity at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> above the earth if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>≪</mo><mi>R</mi></math> (Radius of earth) is given by
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity g' at a height h from the surface of Earth is given by

g'=GM(R+h)2   ...1

Use the method of binomial expansion to obtain the approximate expression for the acceleration due to gravity at a particular height from the surface of the Earth.

g'=GMR21+hR2= GMR21+hR-2≈GMR21-2hR   ...2

The formula to calculate the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the Earth is given by

g=GMR2   ...(3)

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) and simplify to obtain the required relation.

g'g=GMR21-2hRGMR2= 1-2hR⇒g'=g1-2hR

q:
Inside a uniform spherical shell :

(a) The gravitational field is zero.

(b) The gravitational potential is zero.

(c) The gravitational field is the same everywhere.

(d) The gravitation potential is the same everywhere.

(e) All the above.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Inside a uniform spherical shell :

(a) The gravitational field is zero.

(b) The gravitational potential is zero.

(c) The gravitational field is the same everywhere.

(d) The gravitation potential is the same everywhere.

(e) All the above.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
By using graphical representation of gravitational potential due to a uniform spherical shell and radius of the shell, we can see inside the shell the gravitational potential is not zero, and the gravitational potential is same everywhere inside the shell.

 

By using graphical representation of gravitational field due to a uniform spherical shell and radius of the shell, we can see the gravitational field inside the shell is zero.

subject: Physics
q: From a sphere of mass $M$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$, a smaller sphere of radius $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$ is carved out such that the cavity made in the original sphere is between its centre and the periphery (See figure). For the configuration in the figure where the distance between the centre of the original sphere and the removed sphere is 3R, the gravitational force between the two sphere is:
question: From a sphere of mass $M$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$, a smaller sphere of radius $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$ is carved out such that the cavity made in the original sphere is between its centre and the periphery (See figure). For the configuration in the figure where the distance between the centre of the original sphere and the removed sphere is 3R, the gravitational force between the two sphere is:
solution:
Volume of removed sphere
$$
V_{\text {remo }}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)
$$
Volume of the sphere (remaining)
$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{\text {remain }}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3-\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3\left(\frac{1}{8}\right) \\
&=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3\left(\frac{7}{8}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore mass of sphere carved and remaining sphere are at respectively $\frac{1}{8} \mathrm{M}$ and $\frac{7}{8} \mathrm{M}$

Therefore, gravitational force between these two sphere,
$$
F=\frac{G \mathrm{M} m}{r^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{G \frac{7 M}{8} \times \frac{1}{8} M}{(3 R)^2}=\frac{7}{64 \times 9} \frac{G M^2}{R^2} \\
&\simeq \frac{41}{3600} \frac{\mathrm{GM}^2}{\mathrm{R}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The escape velocity of a body on a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The escape velocity of the body on another planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, whose density is four times and radius is half of the planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, is
question: The escape velocity of a body on a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The escape velocity of the body on another planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, whose density is four times and radius is half of the planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, is
solution:
We know that escape velocity is given by,

v=2GMR⇒vAvB=MAMB×RBRA=ρARA3ρBRB3×RBRA=ρARA2ρBRB2=14×41⇒vB=vA=12 km s-1

subject: Physics
q: The escape velocity from a spherical planet $A$ is $10 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. The escape velocity from another planet $B$ whose density and radius are $10 \%$ of those of planet $A$, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: The escape velocity from a spherical planet $A$ is $10 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. The escape velocity from another planet $B$ whose density and radius are $10 \%$ of those of planet $A$, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Escape velocity $v_e = R\sqrt{\frac{8\pi G\rho}{3}} \propto R\sqrt{\rho}$.
Planet B: $\rho_B = 0.1\rho_A$, $R_B = 0.1R_A$.
$\frac{v_B}{v_A} = \frac{0.1R_A\sqrt{0.1\rho_A}}{R_A\sqrt{\rho_A}} = 0.1\sqrt{0.1} = \frac{1}{10\sqrt{10}}$.
$v_B = 10 \times \frac{1}{10\sqrt{10}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$ km/s $= \frac{1000}{\sqrt{10}} = 100\sqrt{10}$ m/s.
